schools.summary: Gill: spoken in the wilderness of Sinai, when the Lord commanded unclean persons be put out of the camp and some were defiled by a dead body and unfit for the passover.
Henry: the divine appointment for the solemn burning of a red heifer to ashes, and the preserving of the ashes for purification.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron
the ordinance of the red heifer
a standing remedy for the defilement of death

schools.summary: Gill: a ceremonial ordinance, founded not on plain reason but in the will of God, intended as a type and shadow of the blood and sacrifice of Christ and the efficacy of that to cleanse from sin; the heifer red, without blemish, never yoked (Heb. 9:13-14).

themes: a red heifer without spot, wherein is no blemish, and upon which never came yoke
a type of Christ in every mark
the shadow of his cleansing blood (Heb. 9:13-14)

schools.summary: Gill: given to Eleazar, not Aaron, that the high priest might not be defiled and hindered in his office, and to inure Eleazar to his work.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sacrifice was superintended by Eleazar, brought without the camp and slain before his face.

themes: give her unto Eleazar the priest... bring her forth without the camp, and one shall slay her
the victim led beyond the holy place
Jesus suffering without the gate (Heb. 13:12)

schools.summary: Gill, with Ainsworth: the blood sprinkled with the finger toward the tabernacle; the finger signified the Spirit of Christ, the 'finger of God,' who takes Christ's blood and sprinkles it on the hearts of his people, freeing them from an evil conscience (Heb. 9:14).
Keil & Delitzsch: Eleazar sprinkled of her blood directly before the tabernacle seven times.

themes: sprinkle of her blood directly before the tabernacle... seven times
the perfect presentation of atoning blood
the Spirit applying Christ's blood to the conscience (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: relays the rabbinic account of the burning at the mount of Olives, the cow bound and laid on a pile of cedar, the priest looking on; the whole creature consumed.
Keil & Delitzsch: one burned the heifer in his sight — skin, flesh, blood, with her dung.

themes: one shall burn the heifer... her skin, and her flesh, and her blood, with her dung
the whole creature consumed, nothing reserved
the entire self-offering of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: these were the same three things used at the cleansing of the leper (Lev. 14:4).
Keil & Delitzsch: cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet cast into the midst of the burning of the heifer.

themes: cedar wood, and hyssop, and scarlet... into the midst of the burning of the heifer
the same emblems as the leper's cleansing (Lev. 14:4)
the purifying power of Christ's blood (Ps. 51:7)

schools.summary: Gill: all who were employed about the heifer, from first to last, were defiled in their garments; the creature was reckoned so impure, though its ashes were for purifying, that whoever handled it needed washing.

themes: the priest shall wash his clothes... and... be unclean until the even
the rite that cleanses defiling its ministers
Christ made sin to cleanse us (2 Cor. 5:21)

schools.summary: Gill: this shows one distinct from him that sprinkled the blood, all concerned in the work being defiled.
Keil & Delitzsch: he that burned her washed his clothes, bathed his flesh, and was unclean until the even.

themes: he that burneth her shall wash his clothes... and shall be unclean until the even
every minister of the cleansing defiled
the cost of dealing with defilement

schools.summary: Gill: great care was taken that the priest who burned the cow be pure, separated seven days, a causeway built over Kidron lest any unseen grave defile; the ashes laid up and kept for the congregation as a water of separation, a purification for sin.
Keil & Delitzsch: a clean man gathered the ashes and laid them up without the camp in a clean place, kept for a water of separation.

themes: a clean man shall gather up the ashes... for a water of separation: it is a purification for sin
one sacrifice of abiding efficacy
Christ offered once, ever fresh to cleanse (Heb. 10:14)

schools.summary: Gill: even the gatherer of the ashes was defiled and must wash his clothes.
Ellicott: the statute reaching the stranger figures that the remission of sins through Christ Jesus was not only to the Jews and their children, but also to all that were afar off (Acts 2:39).

themes: it shall be unto the children of Israel, and unto the stranger... for a statute for ever
the cleansing offered to all alike
the remission in Christ to all afar off (Acts 2:39)

schools.summary: Gill: this is instanced as the principal pollution for which the water of purification was appointed; touching a dead beast defiled only till evening, but a dead man seven days.
Keil & Delitzsch: whoever touched a corpse was unclean seven days.

themes: He that toucheth the dead body of any man shall be unclean seven days
death the chief emblem of sin's pollution
cleansed by the blood that purges dead works (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: he was to purify himself with the water of purification, first on the third day.
Keil & Delitzsch: purified on the third and seventh days; but if not on the third, then not clean on the seventh.

themes: He shall purify himself with it on the third day, and on the seventh day he shall be clean
the third as needful as the seventh
God's cleansing used in his own way and time

schools.summary: Gill: this is of the man who neglects the means God appointed for his cleansing. Calvin: the severity shows how pleasing purity is to God; a forgotten sprinkling might be made up later, but to enter the sanctuary unclean was a capital crime, mixing holy and profane.

themes: Whosoever toucheth the dead... and purifieth not himself, defileth the tabernacle... shall be cut off
how dear purity is to God
the holy not to be confounded with the unclean

schools.summary: Gill: a tent is named because the Israelites then dwelt in tents, but the law holds equally for a house; all who came in and all that was in it were unclean seven days.

themes: when a man dieth in a tent: all that come into the tent... shall be unclean seven days
the pervasiveness of death's pollution
the contagiousness of corruption

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi and Maimonides: this is to be understood of an earthen vessel; the open vessel took the defilement, the covered was preserved.
Keil & Delitzsch: every open vessel which had no covering bound upon it was unclean.

themes: every open vessel, which hath no covering bound upon it, is unclean
the covered vessel kept, the open defiled
the unguarded heart takes the taint of sin

schools.summary: Gill: one slain by the sword or any violent death, or one dead a natural death, or even a bone or a grave, defiled seven days.
Keil & Delitzsch: whosoever touched one slain, a dead body, a bone of a man, or a grave, was unclean seven days.

themes: whosoever toucheth one that is slain... or a dead body, or a bone of a man, or a grave, shall be unclean
death's pollution clinging to its least remains
sin's contagion reaching further than we know

schools.summary: Gill: the ashes of the burnt heifer of purification for sin, with running water put thereto in a vessel; Ellicott: 'the ashes of the burnt sin-offering.'

Wesley: the running water (from a spring or river, the purest) signifies God's Spirit, by which alone true purification is obtained; those who promise themselves Christ's righteousness while submitting not to his Spirit deceive themselves.

themes: the ashes of the burnt heifer of purification for sin, and running water... in a vessel
the virtue of the sacrifice and the Spirit joined
no purifying where the blood is not applied by the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: three stalks of hyssop bound together, the sprinkler being a clean person, not necessarily a priest.
Keil & Delitzsch: he sprinkled it upon the tent, vessels, and persons, and upon him that touched a bone, one slain, one dead, or a grave.

themes: a clean person shall take hyssop, and dip it in the water, and sprinkle it upon... the persons
the lowly herb of purging (Ps. 51:7)
the cleansing applied to all that sin defiled

schools.summary: Gill: the clean priest, or any clean person, sprinkled the water of purification upon the unclean on the third and seventh days.
Ellicott: the clean person purifies the unclean, who then washes himself and is clean at even.

themes: the clean person shall sprinkle upon the unclean on the third day, and on the seventh
the cleansing brought from another and washed through
clean at even, restored to fellowship

schools.summary: Gill: this is of the man unclean by touching any dead body, bone, or grave.
Wesley: 'shall not purify himself' is one who contemptuously refuses to submit to this way of purification.

themes: the man that shall be unclean, and shall not purify himself... shall be cut off
the warning fastened on contempt of the cleansing
to scorn the one cleansing is to perish in it (Heb. 10:29)

schools.summary: Gill: it was a perpetual statute to Israel throughout their generations, until the coming of the Messiah, when the ceremonial law of washings and purifications was abolished; the sprinkler and he that touched the water were themselves made unclean (Heb. 9:10).

themes: he that sprinkleth the water of separation shall wash his clothes... he that toucheth... unclean until even
the cleansing defiling its minister
the shadow standing till the reformation (Heb. 9:10)

schools.summary: Gill: this is of the person unclean by touching a dead body, bone, or grave; whatever he touched was unclean, and the soul that touched that — denoting the spreading nature of pollution.

themes: whatsoever the unclean person toucheth shall be unclean; and the soul that toucheth it shall be unclean
the contagiousness of uncleanness
sin catching and communicative, stopped only by God's cleansing
